questions.questionText: The'index case' in an infectious disease outbreak refers to:
questions.optionB: The first case to come to the attention of health authorities (may not be the primary/source case — primary case is the one who introduces infection into the population)
questions.optionD: The last case in the outbreak
questions.explanation: Core concept: Index case = first identified case; primary case = original source. Why correct: Index case brings the outbreak to notice. Primary case (source case) is the first to introduce infection into the population. They may be different people. Why A wrong: Lab confirmation is not required for index case identification. Why C wrong: Severity is irrelevant to index case definition. Why D wrong: Last case = terminal case of the outbreak. Clinical relevance: Contact tracing starts from the index case. Generation time: time between infection of primary case and secondary case. Serial interval: time between symptom onset in primary and secondary. Exam trap: Index case ≠ primary case → often confused. The person who is first IDENTIFIED (index) may not be first INFECTED (primary). Memory aid: 'Index = I noticed first; Primary = First to catch it.' Reference: Park – Communicable Diseases.
questions.conceptTag: Index case primary case outbreak definition

questions.questionText: The most effective single intervention to prevent diarrheal disease transmission is:
questions.optionB: Handwashing with soap (reduces diarrheal disease by ~40% — most cost-effective single hygiene intervention)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Handwashing with soap is the single most effective intervention against diarrheal diseases. Why correct: Reduces diarrhea incidence by ~40%, respiratory infections by ~23%. Breaks fecal-oral transmission. Most cost-effective hygiene intervention. Critical times: after defecation, before eating, before food preparation, after handling child's feces. Why A wrong: ORS is TREATMENT (prevents dehydration), not prevention of transmission. Why C wrong: Antibiotics — treat specific infections, not primary prevention. Why D wrong: Vaccines (rotavirus, cholera) are important but less universally applicable than handwashing. Clinical relevance: F-diagram: Feces → Fingers/Flies/Fields/Fluids/Fomites → Food → next person. Barriers: sanitation, handwashing, safe water, fly control. Exam trap: ORS is the most important TREATMENT for diarrhea. Handwashing is the most important PREVENTION. Don't mix them up. Memory aid: 'Handwashing = Halts diarrhea at the Hands (40% reduction).' Reference: Park – Communicable Diseases.

questions.questionText: Herd immunity threshold for measles (R0 ≈ 12-18) is approximately:
questions.optionB: 92-95% (Herd immunity threshold = 1 - 1/R0; for measles R0=12-18: HIT = 1 - 1/15 ≈ 93%)
questions.explanation: Core concept: HIT = 1 - 1/R0 → higher R0 requires higher vaccination coverage. Why correct: Measles R0 = 12-18 (one of the most contagious diseases). HIT = 1 - 1/15 = 93.3%. Meaning 93-95% of population must be immune to prevent outbreaks. Why A wrong: 50-60% = adequate for diseases with lower R0 (influenza R0≈2, HIT≈50%). Why C wrong: 70% = adequate for polio (R0≈5-7, HIT≈80-85%). Why D wrong: 30% insufficient for any common VPD. Clinical relevance: Herd immunity protects those who CANNOT be vaccinated (immunocompromised, infants too young). Any drop below HIT → outbreak risk. Anti-vaccination movements threaten herd immunity for measles. Exam trap: R0 is the basic reproduction number — average secondary cases from one case in a fully susceptible population. Higher R0 = more contagious = higher HIT needed. Memory aid: 'Measles = Most contagious = Must have 95% coverage.' Reference: Park – Immunization.
